Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Spices and herbs

**Lemongrass**—also **lemon grass**, **citronella**, and **sereh**[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)*[[2]](#cite_note-:7-2)*[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)—is an aromatic grassy stalk from South and Southeast Asia that is used as an herb.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Growing from the ground, lemongrass occurs as clusters of grassy stalks.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) The stems look somewhat like scallions,[[2]](#cite_note-:7-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4) though they are quite firm and fibrous,[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10) and they have long, rough leaf blades branching off.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) The stem bases may be tinged with purple in addition to their light green and white.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) As the name implies, lemongrass carries a grassy lemony aroma,[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8) which is due largely to its citral content.[[2]](#cite_note-:7-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5) The bottom-most part of the stalk is the most aromatic,[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8) though the leaves and higher-up regions do still have some aroma.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)

The most common variety of lemongrass used in cooking is West Indian lemongrass (C. citratus),[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) but other varieties such as Malabar/East Indian lemongrass (C. flexuosus), Ceylon citronella (C. nardus), and Java citronella (C. winterianus) also exist.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) The different varieties have slightly different flavor profiles due to differing contents of aromatic compounds like citral, citronellal, etc.

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Lemongrass stalks are sold at local markets, farmers markets, and grocery stores, depending on your location.[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10) In areas where lemongrass is not native, it can often be found fresh in specialty Asian grocery stores.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10) The leaves may be trimmed from the stalks to varying degrees. Generally, you want to look for fresh stalks that are plump, firm, green, with no drying or browning.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10) Frozen lemongrass is the next-best option after fresh;[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8) if lemongrass stalks are not available either fresh or frozen, try looking for bottled lemongrass paste.[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)

If the fresh stalks still have leaves attached, trim them before storage.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) The fresh stalks will keep for several weeks in the fridge when wrapped in plastic or a cloth.[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) For longer storage, lemongrass can be frozen in airtight containers[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)—when doing so, it's best to first break it down to the form in which you'll be using it (e.g. small batons, slices, grating).[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) Dried lemongrass should be kept dry, in airtight containers away from light.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

The first step to using lemongrass stalk is removing any leaves from the stalk and peeling off any dry outer layers.[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) Cut off and discard the very bottom root portion, then the top where the stalk gets thin and very green[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)—you should be left with the meatiest and most aromatic pale bottom portion.[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) Next, because the aromatic oils are stored well within the tough plant, the stalks must be broken down at least somewhat before use in order to release the flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3) When using the stalk in chunks, it is typically bashed and bruised to do so before infusing it into the dish.[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) The stalk may also be **very** finely sliced or grated/pounded to a paste,[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:11-10)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) which allows the entire thing to disperse in your dish. Note that the outermost portion of the stalk is typically too tough to eat, so only the more tender core is incorporated into the dish in its entirety.*[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)*[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)[[8]](#cite_note-:9-8)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Overall, lemongrass is used as an aromatic herb in South(east) Asian cooking,[[2]](#cite_note-:7-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) where it flavors anything from soups, stir-fries, tisanes, salads, curry pastes, meat dishes, and more.[[2]](#cite_note-:7-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) Lemongrass soup is a well-known example, with variations in the cuisines of multiple countries (e.g. Thai *tom yum*).[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) The whole stalks may be used as skewers for cooking, which imparts their flavor into the skewered foods.[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)

If working with dried lemongrass, the best application is to simmer it in a dish, which will allow it to rehydrate and slowly perfume the dish.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) The leaves can be used if you would like to—in this case, tie them into a knot for easy removal after infusion.[[3]](#cite_note-:8-3)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Some may suggest lemon zest as a substitute for lemongrass, but doing so will lose some of the subtle aromatic character of lemongrass.
